cry's blog

Codeforces Round 952 (Div. 4) Editorial
By cry, 23 months ago,

This was our first time setting a Div.4 contest. We sincerely hope you enjoyed the problems!

1985A - Creating Words

Problem Credits: cry
Analysis: cry

Solution

To swap the first character of the strings, you can use the built-in method std::swap in C++, or for each string, separate the first character from the rest of the string and concatenate it with the other string.

Code (C++)
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

int main(){
	int t; cin >> t;
	while(t--){
		string a, b; cin >> a >> b;
		swap(a[0], b[0]);
		cout << a << " " << b << endl;
	}
}
1985B - Maximum Multiple Sum

Problem Credits: cry
Analysis: cry

Solution

To maximize the number of multiples of xx less than nn, it optimal to choose a small xx, in this case, 22. The only exception is n=3n=3, where it is optimal to choose 33 instead, since both 22 and 33 have only one multiple less than 33.

Code (C++)
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

int main(){
	int t; cin >> t;
	while(t--){
		int n; cin >> n;
		cout << (n == 3 ? 3 : 2) << endl;
 	}
}
1985C - Good Prefixes

Problem Credits: sum
Analysis: cry

Solution

The only element that can be the sum of all other elements is the maximum element, since all elements are positive. Therefore, for each prefix ii from 11 to nn, check if sum(a1,a2,...,ai)−max(a1,a2,...,ai)=max(a1,a2,...,ai)sum(a1,a2,...,ai)−max(a1,a2,...,ai)=max(a1,a2,...,ai). The sum and max of prefixes can be tracked with variables outside the loop.

Code (C++)
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main(){
	int t; cin >> t;
	while(t--){
		int n; cin >> n;
		int a[n];
		for(int i = 0; i < n; i++)
			cin >> a[i];
		long long sum = 0;
		int mx = 0, ans = 0;;
		for(int i = 0; i < n; i++){
			sum += a[i];
			mx = max(mx, a[i]);
			if(sum - mx == mx) 
				ans++;
		}
		cout << ans << endl;
	}
}
1985D - Manhattan Circle

Problem Credits: cry
Analysis: cry

Solution

Note that the manhattan circle is always in a diamond shape, symmetric from the center. Let's take notice of some special characteristics that can help us. One way is to find the top and bottom points of the circle. Note that these points will have columns at the center of the circle, so here we can acquire the value of kk. To find hh, since the circle is symmetric, it is just the middle of the rows of the top and bottom points.

Note that we never needed to find the value of rr.

Code (C++)
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
const int INF = 1e9;

int main(){
	int t; cin >> t;
	while(t--){
		int n, m; cin >> n >> m;
		vector<vector<char>> g(n, vector<char>(m));
		for(int i = 0; i < n; i++){
			for(int j = 0; j < m; j++){
				cin >> g[i][j];
			}
		}
		pair<int, int> top = {INF, INF}, bottom = {-INF, -INF};
		for(int i = 0; i < n; i++){
			for(int j = 0; j < m; j++){
				if(g[i][j] == '#'){
					top = min(top, {i, j});
					bottom = max(bottom, {i, j});
				}
			}
		}
		assert(top.second == bottom.second);
		cout << (top.first + bottom.first) / 2 + 1 << " " << top.second + 1 << endl;
	}
}
1985E - Secret Box

Problem Credits: cry
Analysis: cry

Solution

Since the side lengths of SS has to multiply to kk, all three side lengths of SS has to be divisors of kk. Let's denote the side lengths of SS along the xx, yy, and zz axes as aa, bb, and cc respectively. For SS to fit in BB , a≤xa≤x, b≤yb≤y, and c≤zc≤z must hold. Because of the low constraints, we can afford to loop through all possible values of aa and bb, and deduce that c=ka⋅bc=ka⋅b (make sure c≤zc≤z and cc is an integer). To get the amount of ways we can place SS, we can just multiply the amount of shifting space along each axes, and that just comes down to (x−a+1)⋅(y−b+1)⋅(z−c+1)(x−a+1)⋅(y−b+1)⋅(z−c+1). The answer is the maximum among all possible values of aa, bb, and cc .

The time complexity is O(n2)O(n2) where nn is at most 20002000.

Code (C++)
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;
using ll = long long;

int main(){
	int t; cin >> t;
	while(t--){
		ll x, y, z, k; cin >> x >> y >> z >> k;
		ll ans = 0;
		for(int a = 1; a <= x; a++){
			for(int b = 1; b <= y; b++){
				if(k % (a * b)) continue;
				ll c = k / (a * b);
				if(c > z) continue;
				ll ways = (ll)(x - a + 1) * (y - b + 1) * (z - c + 1);
				ans = max(ans, ways);
			}
		}
		cout << ans << "\n";
	}
}
1985F - Final Boss

Problem Credits: cry, sum
Analysis: cry, sum

About Hacks

Unfortunately, there was a lot of hacks on this problem, and we're sorry for it. Since our intended solution is not binary search, we didn't really take overflow using binary search seriously. I (cry) prepared this problem and I only took into account about overflow with big cooldown, but I forgot overflow can happen on attacks as well. I apologize and we will do better next time!

Solution

Since the sum of hh is bounded by 2⋅1052⋅105, and each attack deals at least 11 damage. If we assume every turn we can make at least one attack, the sum of turns to kill the boss in every test case is bounded by 2⋅1052⋅105. This means that we can afford to simulate each turn where we make at least one attack.

But what if we cannot make an attack on this turn? Since the cooldown for each attack can be big, we cannot increment turns one by one. We must jump to the next turn we can make an attack. This can be done by retrieving the first element of a sorted set, where the set stores pairs {tt, ii} which means {next available turn you can use this attack, index of this attack} for all ii. Here, we can set the current turn to tt and use all attacks in the set with first element in the pair equal to tt. Remember to insert the pair to {ci+tci+t, ii} back into the set after processing the attacks.

The time complexity is O(hlogn)O(hlog⁡n).

Code (C++)
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

int main(){
	int t; cin >> t;
	while(t--){
		int h, n; cin >> h >> n;
		vector<int> a(n), c(n);
		for(int& i: a) cin >> i;
		for(int& i: c) cin >> i;
		set<pair<long long, int>> S;
		for(int i = 0; i < n; i++){
			S.insert({1, i});
		}
		long long last_turn = 1;
		while(h > 0){
			auto [turn, i] = *S.begin();
			S.erase(S.begin());
			last_turn = turn;
			h -= a[i];
			S.insert({turn + c[i], i});
		}
		cout << last_turn << "\n";
	}
}

// comment "tomato" if you see this comment
Bonus

Try to solve this if 1≤h≤1091≤h≤109.

Solution

We can do this by binary searching for the answer. For some time tt, we know that we can perform an attack of cooldown cc exactly ⌊t−1c⌋+1⌊t−1c⌋+1 times. The total damage we will do in time tt will be:

∑i=1nai(⌊t−1ci⌋+1)∑i=1nai(⌊t−1ci⌋+1)

So we binary search for the first tt such that the total damage we do in time tt is greater than or equal to hh. This runs in O(nlog(h⋅maxci))O(nlog⁡(h⋅maxci)). Be careful of long long overflow!

Code (C++)
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

#define ll long long

void solve(){
    ll h, n;
    cin >> h >> n;

    vector<ll> A(n), C(n);

    for (ll &i : A)
        cin >> i;
    for (ll &i : C)
        cin >> i;
    
    auto chk = [&](ll t){
        ll dmg = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < n and dmg < h; i++){
            ll cnt = (t - 1) / C[i] + 1;

            if (cnt >= h)
                return true;

            dmg += cnt * A[i];
        }
        return dmg >= h;
    };

    ll L = 1, H = 1e12;

    while (L < H){
        ll M = (L + H) / 2;
        chk(M) ? H = M : L = M + 1;
    }
    cout << L << "\n";
}

int main(){
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0); cin.tie(0); 
    int tc;
    cin >> tc;

    while (tc--)
        solve();
}
1985G - D-Function

Problem Credits: cry
Analysis: cry

Solution

To satisfy D(k⋅n)=k⋅D(n)D(k⋅n)=k⋅D(n), each digit dd in nn must become k⋅dk⋅d after multiplying nn by kk. In other words, none of nn's digits can carry over to the next digit upon multiplication. From this, we can deduce that each digit in nn must be less than or equal to ⌊9k⌋⌊9k⌋. Only thing left is to count all such numbers in the range of 10l10l inclusive and 10r10r exclusive.

Every number below 10r10r has rr or less digits. For numbers with less than rr digits, let's pad the beginning with zeroes until it becomes a rr digit number (for example, if r=5r=5, then 6969 becomes 0006900069). This allows us to consider numbers with less than rr digits the same way as numbers with exactly rr digits. For each digit, we have ⌊9k⌋+1⌊9k⌋+1 choices (including zero), and there are rr digits, so the total number of numbers that satisfies the constraint below 10r10r is (⌊9k⌋+1)r(⌊9k⌋+1)r.

To get the count of numbers in range, it suffices to subtract all valid numbers less than 10l10l. Therefore, the answer is (⌊9k⌋+1)r−(⌊9k⌋+1)l(⌊9k⌋+1)r−(⌊9k⌋+1)l. To exponentiate fast, we can use modular exponentiation.

Code (Python)
MOD = int(1e9+7)
t = int(input())
for _ in range(t):
    l, r, k = map(int, input().split())
    print((pow(9 // k + 1, r, MOD) - pow(9 // k + 1, l, MOD) + MOD) % MOD)
1985H1 - Maximize the Largest Component (Easy Version)

Problem Credits: sum
Analysis: sum

Solution

Let's first solve the problem if we can only select and fill rows. Columns can be handled in the exact same way.

For each row rr, we need to find the size of the component formed by filling row rr (i.e. the size of the component containing row rr if we set all cells in row rr to be ##).

The size of the component containing row rr if we set all cells in row rr to be ## will be the sum of:

  1. The number of .. in row rr since these cells will be set to ##. Let FrFr denote this value for some row rr.
  2. The sum of sizes of components containing a cell in either row r−1r−1, rr, or r+1r+1 (i.e. components that are touching row rr). This is since these components will be part of the component containing row rr. Let RrRr denote this value for some row rr.

The challenge is computing the second term quickly. For some component, let ss be the size of the component and let rminrmin and rmaxrmax denote the minimum and maximum row of a cell in the component. This means that the component will contain cells with rows rmin,rmin+1,...,rmaxrmin,rmin+1,...,rmax. Note that we can find these values with a dfs. Since the component will contribute ss to rows in [rmin−1,rmin,…rmax+1][rmin−1,rmin,…rmax+1], we add ss to Rrmin−1,Rrmin,…,Rrmax+1Rrmin−1,Rrmin,…,Rrmax+1. This can be done naively or with prefix sums.

We find the maximum Fr+RrFr+Rr and then handle columns in the same way. This solution runs in O(nm)O(nm) time.

Code (C++)
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

int n, m, minR, maxR, minC, maxC, sz, ans; vector<int> R, C, freeR, freeC; 
vector<vector<bool>> vis; vector<vector<char>> A;

void dfs(int i, int j){
    if (i <= 0 or i > n or j <= 0 or j > m or vis[i][j] or A[i][j] == '.')
        return;
    
    vis[i][j] = true;

    sz++;
    minR = min(minR, i);
    maxR = max(maxR, i);
    minC = min(minC, j);
    maxC = max(maxC, j);

    dfs(i - 1, j);
    dfs(i + 1, j);
    dfs(i, j - 1);
    dfs(i, j + 1);
}

void solve(){
    cin >> n >> m;

    R.assign(n + 5, 0);
    C.assign(m + 5, 0);
    freeR.assign(n + 5, 0);
    freeC.assign(m + 5, 0);
    vis.assign(n + 5, vector<bool>(m + 5, false));
    A.assign(n + 5, vector<char>(m + 5, ' '));

    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++){
        for (int j = 1; j <= m; j++){
            cin >> A[i][j];

            if (A[i][j] == '.'){
                freeR[i]++;
                freeC[j]++;
            }
        }
    }
    
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++){
        for (int j = 1; j <= m; j++){
            if (vis[i][j] or A[i][j] == '.')
                continue;

            // Reset
            sz = 0;
            minR = 1e9;
            maxR = -1e9;
            minC = 1e9;
            maxC = -1e9;

            dfs(i, j);

            // Expand by 1 since adjacent cells also connect
            minR = max(minR - 1, 1);
            maxR = min(maxR + 1, n);
            minC = max(minC - 1, 1);
            maxC = min(maxC + 1, m);
            
            // Update prefix sums
            R[minR] += sz;
            R[maxR + 1] -= sz;

            C[minC] += sz;
            C[maxC + 1] -= sz;
        }
    }

    ans = 0;

    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++){
        R[i] += R[i - 1];
        ans = max(ans, freeR[i] + R[i]);
    }
    for (int i = 1; i <= m; i++){
        C[i] += C[i - 1];
        ans = max(ans, freeC[i] + C[i]);
    }

    cout << ans << "\n";
}

int main(){
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0); cin.tie(0);
    int tc;
    cin >> tc;

    while (tc--)
        solve();
}
1985H2 - Maximize the Largest Component (Hard Version)

Problem Credits: sum
Analysis: sum

Solution

For each row rr and column cc, we need to find the size of the component formed by filling both row rr and column cc (i.e. the size of the component containing row rr and column cc if we set all cells in both row rr and column cc to be ##).

Extending the reasoning in H1, for some row rr and column cc, consider the sum of:

  1. The number of .. in row rr or column cc since these cells will be set to ##. Let Fr,cFr,c denote this value for some row rr and column cc.
  2. The sum of sizes of components containing a cell in either row r−1r−1, rr, or r+1r+1 (i.e. components that are touching row rr). This is since these components will be part of the component containing row rr and column cc. Let RrRr denote this value for some row rr.
  3. The sum of sizes of components containing a cell in either column c−1c−1, cc, or c+1c+1 (i.e. components that are touching column cc). This is since these components will be part of the component containing row rr and column cc. Let CcCc denote this value for some column cc.

However, components that contain a cell in either row r−1r−1, rr, or r+1r+1 as well as in either column c−1c−1, cc, or c+1c+1 will be overcounted (since it will be counted in both terms 22 and 33) (you can think of it as components touching both row rr and column cc). Thus, we need to subtract the sum of sizes of components that contain a cell in either row r−1r−1, rr, or r+1r+1 as well as in either column c−1c−1, cc, or c+1c+1. Let Br,cBr,c denote this for some row rr and column cc.

Then the size of the component formed by filling both row rr and column cc will be Fr,c+Rr+Cc−Br,cFr,c+Rr+Cc−Br,c and we want to find the maximum value of this.

Let's try to calculate these values efficiently. Consider some component.

  1. Let ss be its size.
  2. Let rminrmin and rmaxrmax denote the minimum and maximum row of a cell in the component. This means that the component contains cells with rows rmin,rmin+1,...,rmaxrmin,rmin+1,...,rmax.
  3. Let cmincmin and cmaxcmax denote the minimum and maximum column of a cell in the component. This means that the component contains cells with columns cmin,cmin+1,...,cmaxcmin,cmin+1,...,cmax.

All these values can be found with a dfs. We then do the following updates:

  1. Add ss to Rrmin−1,Rrmin,…,Rrmax+1Rrmin−1,Rrmin,…,Rrmax+1. This can be done naively or with prefix sums.
  2. Add ss to Ccmin−1,Ccmin,…,Ccmax+1Ccmin−1,Ccmin,…,Ccmax+1. This can be done naively or with prefix sums.
  3. Add ss to the subrectangle of BB with top left at (rmin−1,cmin−1rmin−1,cmin−1) and bottom right at (rmax+1,cmax+1rmax+1,cmax+1). This can be done with 2D prefix sums. (Note that doing this naively will pass because of low constant factor and the fact that we could not cut this solution without cutting slow correct solutions.)

We do this for each component. Also, calculating Fr,cFr,c can be done by looking at the number of .. in row rr, column cc, and checking whether we overcounted a .. at (r,cr,c). In all, this solution runs in O(nm)O(nm) time.

Code (C++)
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

int n, m, minR, maxR, minC, maxC, sz, ans; vector<int> R, C, freeR, freeC; 
vector<vector<int>> RC; vector<vector<bool>> vis; vector<vector<char>> A;

void dfs(int i, int j){
    if (i <= 0 or i > n or j <= 0 or j > m or vis[i][j] or A[i][j] == '.')
        return;
    
    vis[i][j] = true;

    sz++;
    minR = min(minR, i);
    maxR = max(maxR, i);
    minC = min(minC, j);
    maxC = max(maxC, j);

    dfs(i - 1, j);
    dfs(i + 1, j);
    dfs(i, j - 1);
    dfs(i, j + 1);
}

void solve(){
    cin >> n >> m;

    R.assign(n + 5, 0);
    C.assign(m + 5, 0);
    freeR.assign(n + 5, 0);
    freeC.assign(m + 5, 0);
    RC.assign(n + 5, vector<int>(m + 5, 0));
    vis.assign(n + 5, vector<bool>(m + 5, false));
    A.assign(n + 5, vector<char>(m + 5, ' '));

    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++){
        for (int j = 1; j <= m; j++){
            cin >> A[i][j];

            if (A[i][j] == '.'){
                freeR[i]++;
                freeC[j]++;
            }
        }
    }
    
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++){
        for (int j = 1; j <= m; j++){
            if (vis[i][j] or A[i][j] == '.')
                continue;

            // Reset
            sz = 0;
            minR = 1e9;
            maxR = -1e9;
            minC = 1e9;
            maxC = -1e9;

            dfs(i, j);

            // Expand by 1 since adjacent cells also connect
            minR = max(minR - 1, 1);
            maxR = min(maxR + 1, n);
            minC = max(minC - 1, 1);
            maxC = min(maxC + 1, m);
            
            // Update prefix sums
            R[minR] += sz;
            R[maxR + 1] -= sz;

            C[minC] += sz;
            C[maxC + 1] -= sz;

            RC[minR][minC] += sz;
            RC[maxR + 1][minC] -= sz;
            RC[minR][maxC + 1] -= sz;
            RC[maxR + 1][maxC + 1] += sz;
        }
    }

    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++)
        R[i] += R[i - 1];

    for (int i = 1; i <= m; i++)
        C[i] += C[i - 1];

    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++)
        for (int j = 1; j <= m; j++)
            RC[i][j] += RC[i - 1][j] + RC[i][j - 1] - RC[i - 1][j - 1];
    
    ans = 0;

    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++)
        for (int j = 1; j <= m; j++)
            ans = max(ans, (R[i] + C[j] - RC[i][j]) + (freeR[i] + freeC[j] - (A[i][j] == '.')));
    
    cout << ans << "\n";
}

int main(){
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0); cin.tie(0);
    int tc;
    cin >> tc;

    while (tc--)
        solve();
}

    how to check overflow, please tell the constraints on left and right, and did you do l<=r or l < r, can you please explain

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      mahendraakshansh
      22 months ago, show (+4) #
      »
      »
      »
      mahendraakshansh
      22 months ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      Thats not the overflow error. In binary search many including me put r as 1e12 ish.

      The max value of attack will be — 1e5 * 1e5 * 1e12.(N, attack[i], mid) long long cant handle that. To solve it, at every iteration of n we have to check if the value is greater than health or not

lptd
22 months ago, show (+2) #

lptd
22 months ago, hide # |
 
0

What is the difference between my binary search sol and those that got hacked? I wanna know more on why I got pass the hacking phase.
https://codeforces.com/contest/1985/submission/265355482

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    Ryuu7
    22 months ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    Ryuu7
    22 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    It's when someone uses a very high value for the upper limit of binary search (like 1e18). Then (turns/c[i])*a[i] could go very high, even above 64 bit long long. So to AC you could either use int128, or set a lower upper limit such as yours. Or use a lang like python :)

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      Naman1213
      22 months ago, show #
      »
      »
      »
      Naman1213
      22 months ago, hide # ^ |
      ← Rev. 2 →  
      +6

      You're wrong, it's not because of the upper bound you take. I've also taken 1e11 as upper bound but got hacked. 265328896

      If all attacks have damage 2e5 and cooldown is 1, sum can reach 2e5 x 2e5 x mid. if mid is >= 1e8, it overflows. Yours is correct because of the h -= a[i]. It prevents running binary search in such cases.

      The correct way to deal with this is to use 128 bit integer or break out of the loop as soon as sum becomes >= h.

      → Reply
»
Aspergillus
22 months ago, show (+9) #
»
Aspergillus
22 months ago, hide # |
 
0

Anyone who used DSU for H1,H2 explain your solution?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    SNX03
    22 months ago, show (+7) #
    »
    »
    SNX03
    22 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +3

    You can use dsu to join components of '#'. Notice that for any (n∗m)(n∗m) grid, we can represent (i,j)(i,j) as (i∗m+j)(i∗m+j). So initialize a disjoint set of (n∗m)(n∗m) components at first.

    Then whenever you encounter a '#', you can run a flood fill via dfs / bfs and take (i,j)(i,j) = (i∗m+j)(i∗m+j) as the parent for all '#' you encounter.

    Now you have size of all the connected components of '#'.

    Now you can either '#' an entire row or column. I'm considering row here, same thing can be done for each column.

    Notice when you '#' an entire row, You already have some '#' as parents, once you '#' the row, all these parents combine together.

    One more thing, the parents from row above and row below will also be joined in this new component.

    Hence the final answer would be Count(′.′)Count(′.′) + size of each parent you encounter.

    To prevent taking a parent twice you can use the set.

    Code:

    Spoiler

    struct dsu {
    vector<int> p, sz;
    vector<pair<int, int>> query;
    int comp;
    dsu(int n) {
        comp = n;
        query.resize(n);
        p.resize(n); sz.resize(n, 1);
        for(int i=0; i<n; i++) {
            p[i] = i;
            query[i] = {i, i};
        }
    }
 
    int get(int a) {
        return p[a] = (p[a]==a?a:get(p[a]));
    }
    bool same(int a, int b) {
        return get(a) == get(b);
    };
    void unite(int a, int b) {
        a = get(a), b = get(b);
        if(a==b) return;
        if(sz[a] < sz[b]) swap(a, b);
        p[b] = a;
        sz[a] += sz[b];
        query[a].first = max(query[a].first, query[b].first);
        query[a].second = min(query[a].second, query[b].second);
        comp--;
    }
};
 
void solve() {
    cin >> n >> m;
    string s[n];
    rep(i, n) cin >> s[i];
 
    dsu d(n*m);
 
    auto getId = [&](int i, int j) {
        return i*m + j;
    };
    vvi seen(n, vi(m, 0));
    rep(i, n) rep(j, m) {
        if(s[i][j] == '#') {
            queue<pii> q;
            seen[i][j] = 1;
            q.push(mpr(i, j));
            int V = d.get(getId(i, j));
            while(!q.empty()) {
                auto [x, y] = q.front(); q.pop();
                rep(k, 4) {
                    int xi = x + dx[k], yi = y + dy[k];
                    if(xi>=0&&xi<n&&yi>=0&&yi<m&&!seen[xi][yi]&&s[xi][yi]=='#') {
                        q.push(mpr(xi, yi));
                        seen[xi][yi] = 1;
                        d.unite(V, getId(xi, yi));
                    }
                }
            }
        }
    }
   
    ll ans = *max_element(all(d.sz));
 
    rep(i, n) {
        ll cnt = count(all(s[i]), '.');
        tsi ita;
        rep(j, m) if(s[i][j] == '#') ita.ins(d.get(getId(i, j)));
        if(i-1>=0) {
            rep(j, m) if(s[i-1][j] == '#') ita.ins(d.get(getId(i-1, j)));
        }
        if(i+1<n) {
            rep(j, m) if(s[i+1][j] == '#') ita.ins(d.get(getId(i+1, j)));
 
        }
        for(auto x: ita) cnt += d.sz[x];
        chkmax(ans, cnt);
    }
    vi uler(m);
    // debug(d.sz)
    rep(j, m) {
        ll cnt = 0;
        rep(i, n) cnt += (s[i][j] =='.');
        debug(cnt)
        tsi ita;
        rep(i, n) if(s[i][j] == '#') ita.ins(d.get(getId(i, j)));
        if(j-1>=0) {
            rep(i, n) if(s[i][j-1] == '#') ita.ins(d.get(getId(i, j-1)));
        }
        if(j+1<m) {
            rep(i, n) if(s[i][j+1] == '#') ita.ins(d.get(getId(i, j+1)));
 
        }
        debug(ita)
        for(auto x: ita) cnt += d.sz[x];
        chkmax(ans, cnt);
    }
    print(ans);
}
    → Reply

        YourSistersHugeKidneys

        ← Rev. 6 →  
        +3

        Your code have overflow because your arrays parent and Size are too small when you want to iterate from 1, no 0 (but generally you want to have some reserve). Quick fix is setting MAX_N=4e6,also you can change i*m+j to i.e.(i-1)*m+j-1. On the other hand, i suggest to cin whole string rather than char (this is faster)

        string a[n+1];
for(int i=1;i<=n;i++){
    for(int j=1;j<=m;j++){
        cin>>a[i];

        (but then you iterate from 0), and you have a lot of 'shadow declaration' — your i.

        I wonder why you don't have SF

        → Reply

    I use DSU+DP to calculate four arrays: when the operation is at row=x, col=y, what's the total size we can get for the top-left/top-right/bot-left/bot-right w.r.t to (x, y).

    I use set to track rather than unordered_set so the complexity is O(mnlog(n)): https://codeforces.com/contest/1985/submission/266763301

    0

    Consider 2e5 values equal 2e5 in array a, array c : 2e5 values equal 1 .. so, when multiplying (mid / c[i] * a[i]) 1e12 * 2e5 * 2e5 gives 1e22 which doesn't fit into 64bit :)

I'm still eager to see a proof of why there are no such nn that satisfy D(n⋅k)=k⋅D(n)D(n⋅k)=k⋅D(n) in the case of possible carries in multiplication.

  • »

    kefolen
    22 months ago, show #
    »
    »
    kefolen
    22 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    Yeah, guessed it is well, 'cause haven't managed to construct a formal proof of that fact in a reasonable amount of time during the contests. Wonder if such even exists. If yes, I am interested to see it too.

    → Reply
  • »
    »
    anamnesis
    22 months ago, show #
    »
    »
    anamnesis
    22 months ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 4 →  
    +7

    We can see that for a number with more than one digit, its DD will be smaller than itself(this is how number expression works!). For a number with exactly one digit, its DD is equal to itself.

    Imagine we are multiplying a single digit xx by kk, resulting in a carryover. We get a result kxkx which has more than one digit. And we expect D(kx)D(kx) to be kD(x)kD(x), which is equal to kxkx (x=D(x)x=D(x)). Thus, our DD has been smaller than the target. It’s impossible to compensate for this with another digit multiplication since D(ky)D(ky) won’t be greater than kyky for any yy.

    → Reply

    »
    Petertromso
    22 months ago, show #

    0

    For each carry, D will be reduced by 9: the carry will add 1 to D instead of adding ten.

Can anyone help explain why this equation is wrong only with large numbers in problem F using the binary search method?

The number of times we use the current attack is ceil(mid / (c[i] + 1)).

(c[i] + 1) is the segment length in which we can perform only one attack.

Dividing gives us the number of segments we have, and any float will be ceiled. I don't understand why this is incorrect. Can anyone clarify?

Is there a formal proof for B?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    notakshattahskaton
    22 months ago, show (+2) #
    »
    »
    notakshattahskaton
    22 months ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 8 →  
    +14

    Let's consider any number greater than n>3n>3, then for x=2x=2 we have k=⌊n/2⌋k=⌊n/2⌋ , hence 2⋅(1+2+…k)=k⋅(k+1)2⋅(1+2+…k)=k⋅(k+1), which is n⋅(n+2)/4n⋅(n+2)/4 for even nn and (n2−1)/4(n2−1)/4 for odd nn.

    Consider any x≥2x≥2, then, g(x)=(n+1)⋅(n+1−x)2x≤x⋅k⋅(k+1)2≤n⋅(n/x+1)2=n⋅(n+x)2x=f(x)g(x)=(n+1)⋅(n+1−x)2x≤x⋅k⋅(k+1)2≤n⋅(n/x+1)2=n⋅(n+x)2x=f(x). On evaluating, we get f′(x)=−n22x2f′(x)=−n22x2 and g′(x)=−(n+1)22x2g′(x)=−(n+1)22x2, hence a decreasing function. Thus, f(x)f(x) or g(x)g(x) should be maximum at x=2x=2.

    If the f(x)f(x) and g(x)g(x) logic is not convincing, you can think that ⌊n/x⌋⌊n/x⌋ will be always of the form (n−λ)/x(n−λ)/x ,where 0≤λ<x0≤λ<x and it depends on nn. [In fact, λ=n%xλ=n%x] Thus the sum h(x,λ)=(n−λ)⋅(n−λ+x)2xh(x,λ)=(n−λ)⋅(n−λ+x)2x, where λλ itself depends on xx and nn. But, the for the extreme values of λλ, the function h(x)h(x) essentially takes the form f(x)f(x) or g(x)g(x), and it can be shown that it will work for any intermediate λλ as well.

    PS: I am not sure why the above explanation doesn't work for n=3n=3, could anyone point it out? Thanks!

    → Reply

ibrm

+3

write proof for G, please

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    Aspergillus
    22 months ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    Aspergillus
    22 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +4

    If I add two numbers and their digit carry over, then the digit sum of the new number will onviously decrease than the sum of the digit sum of the first two numbers. Same thing for k additions, if it is same after k additions, there should still be no carry. If there is no carry then each digit can be calculated for separately. More precicely each digit can range from 0 to 9/k. If a number is less than 10^l, then there are l digits to be filled so 9/k+1)^l. since we are asked in between, we can simply subtract the two values for r and l.

    → Reply

H2: We find some O(n3)O(n3)(O(nm×min(n,m))O(nm×min(n,m))) solutions like 265329368. Their solutions need to enumerate each cells in the smallest rectangle containing the connected blocks.

The data like

#.#.#.#
..#.#.#
###.#.#
....#.#
#####.#
......#
#######

or 

##.##.##.##
.##.##.##.#
#.##.##.##.
##.##.##.##
.##.##.##.#
#.##.##.##.
##.##.##.##

can let them have O(n3)O(n3), but in fact they just need to run about n312n312 times. We tried another construction, but it also failed. I think these solutions are wrong, hope the new construction can hack them.

→ Reply

Doloria

Doloria
22 months ago, hide # |
 
+3

Hey Guys, did anyone solve E. Secret Box in less than O(xy)?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    SKnight4
    22 months ago, show (+3) #
    »
    »
    SKnight4
    22 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    Yes, it is solvable in O(k).

    Here is the complete solution. Find all divisors of k and store them in a vector(let's call it div). The maximum size of this vector is sqrt(k) (let's call it size sz). Then you can brute force for x and y in this vector and calculate x = k/x/y. Now if these x,y,z satisfy given constraint then you got a valid dimension.

    Overall complexity(O(sz*sz) where sz = sqrt(k)) => so O(k) Here is my O(n) submission: https://codeforces.com/contest/1985/submission/265647792

    → Reply

      »
      »
      Doloria
      22 months ago, show (+2) #

      »
      »
      Doloria
      22 months ago, hide # ^ |
      ← Rev. 2 →  
      0

        »
        »
        »
        SKnight4
        22 months ago, show (+1) #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        SKnight4
        22 months ago, hide # ^ |
         
        0

        Yes, it will still work because number of divisors for k <= 1e9 at max is 1344. So O(1344^2)

          22 months ago, hide # ^ |
           
          0

          in Line 5: 

          double totalDamage = 0;

          He used double to store the totalDamage,so there's no worry about overflow.

          in Line 7:

          double numAttacks = (turns + cooldown[i] - 1) / cooldown[i];

          the expression at right is at most 1e18 , also not overflow.

    You can downgrade it from 3D to 2D, so if you can solve 2D version in constant time then you can solve 3D in linear time. In my opinion, the problem requires you to fix the shape first which I think may not be possible to have a solution faster than O(min(min(x,y),k−−√))O(min(min(x,y),k)).

For problem E,
Can any one explain for h<109h<109, why the given time complexity holds true? Why in loglog there is h∗maxcih∗maxci?
Thanks.

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    1xx55
    22 months ago, show #
    »
    »
    1xx55
    22 months ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    0

    h∗maxcih∗maxci is one upper_bound of turns to kill the boss.

    It's easy to prove. If you only use the attack that have max cooldown time of all attacks , assume that this attack only takes 1 damage , after (h−1)∗maxci+1(h−1)∗maxci+1 turns the boss will die. This is the worst case, so you know that in (h−1)∗maxci+1(h−1)∗maxci+1 turns the boss must die. For (h−1)∗maxci+1<h∗maxci(h−1)∗maxci+1<h∗maxci ,in convinient we use h∗maxcih∗maxci for the upper_bound.

    loglog comes from binary_search approach.We use binary_search to find answer. You can find its time complexity proof on the Internet.

    → Reply
»

G:(D-Function) Can someone explain, why do we add mod in this print((pow(9 // k + 1, r, MOD) - pow(9 // k + 1, l, MOD) + MOD) % MOD) to the result of the difference?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    1xx55
    22 months ago, show #
    »
    »
    1xx55
    22 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    we know that pow(9 // k + 1, r, MOD) ∈[0,MOD−1]∈[0,MOD−1] and pow(9 // k + 1, l, MOD) ∈[0,MOD−1]∈[0,MOD−1]

    so it's possible that in some cases we have (pow(9 // k + 1, r, MOD) < pow(9 // k + 1, l, MOD)

    To avoid print a negative value , we add a MOD to the result.

    → Reply
»

For problem G, how to prove the legal number n should be only that each digits of n multiple k have no influnance to the next digits.

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    1xx55
    22 months ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    1xx55
    22 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +8

    using D(x+y)=D(x)+D(y)−9∗carryD(x+y)=D(x)+D(y)−9∗carry

    To prove this formula , you can start with one digit , and it's correct. And you can expand this to all digits.

    so we have D(k∗n)=k∗D(n)−9∗carriesD(k∗n)=k∗D(n)−9∗carries .

    → Reply

For problem F,
Can anyone help me to understand how ⌊t−1c⌋⌊t−1c⌋ is derived?

Also, please let me know if it is a standard or generic way to calculate in such situations.
Thank you in advance.

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    jay_1410
    22 months ago, show #
    »
    »
    jay_1410
    22 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0 
    say c = Cool Down , t = Turns

now attacks will be on the turns : 1 , 1+c , 1+2c , 1+3c . . . 
so , kth attack will be on the turn : 1 + (k-1) * c

say , till 't' turn , 'k' attacks has been done
so => turn on kth attack <= 't' turn
   => 1 + (k-1) * c <= t
   => k <= (t-1)/c + 1
   => k = (t-1)/c + 1 (since division gives floor result, so we can just equate)
    → Reply
»

For H1 I am using a map of pairs called compNum which basically marks each '**#**' with its component number. I have taken key pairs because I need to store coordinate of '**#**' and the value of this map is component number. And at the same time using another unordered map<int,int>cnt I am counting the size of the current component number and using a visited matrix I am keeping track of whether the neighboring '**#**' is visited or not, standard dfs stuff. Then for each row I am placing '**#**' and counting answers as follows: FOr each row ri, if the cell contains '**#**' then using a map I am checking whether the component to which this cell belong to has already taken or not. The compNum map gives the component of this cell. And if it is not '**#**' then I increment the current answer by 1 because I place a new '**#**' in place of '**.**' and now I check four neighbors if any neighbor is '**#**' and its component is not taken then I add the size of the component to my current answer and proceed. Finally when I am done computing the value of the current answer for a row then I try to maximize my final ans with the current answer same stuff I do for columns. But I don't know why it is giving TLE. Any help will be appreciated. Thank you for spending time in understanding my approach.

In the problem G floor(9/k) + 1 why we are adding +1

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    ZombieUser
    22 months ago, show #
    »
    »
    ZombieUser
    22 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    floor(9/k)+1 is helping us find the number of distinct digits d we can place in a single position without making k*d overflow 9.

    For example for k=4, possible no. of d are 9/4+1=3 and these are d=0(4*0<=9),d=1(4*1<=9) and d=2(4*2<=9). That +1 is for the digit 0.

    → Reply
»
ash1596
22 months ago, show (+1) #
»

22 months ago, hide # |

H1 : Can some explain this part?

            // Update prefix sums
            R[minR] += sz;
            R[maxR + 1] -= sz;

            C[minC] += sz;
            C[maxC + 1] -= sz;
→ Reply
  • »
    »
    uleenneelu
    22 months ago, show #
    »
    »
    uleenneelu
    22 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    If you need to add x to a range (i,j) in an array, you can iterate from i to j and add x to each array element. This would be in the O(n). Now if you have m such operations, it will be O(m*n).

    So a better way to do this is to use this prefix array trick. To add x in (i,j), you can add x to pre[i], and subtract x from pre[j+1]. Now if you make the prefix sum array of this array, it will give you a value for each index that's added to each corresponding array element.

    e.g: arr[]={0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0} i=1, j=3 (0-indexing) After operation: arr[]={0, 1, 0, 0, -1, 0} The prefix sum array: {0, 1, 1, 1, 0, 0}

    → Reply
»

For H2, Note that doing this naively will pass because of low constant factor and the fact that we could not cut this solution without cutting slow correct solutions

I believe the following test case will blow up naive calculations for the subrectangle of B

Test:

Spoiler
1
10 10
#.#.#.#.#.
..#.#.#.#.
###.#.#.#.
....#.#.#.
#####.#.#.
......#.#.
#######.#.
........#.
#########.
..........

Pseudo code:

    int n = 1000;
    out(n, n);
    vector<string> s(n, string(n, '.'));
    forn(d, n){
        forn(i, n) if(i <= d) s[i][d] = s[d][i] = '#';
        d++; // alternate . vs #
    }
    forn(i, n) out(s[i]);

Result: Prefix sum solution 277004120 = 400ms. Naive solution 277003713 = 3400ms (in practice AC with 400ms since this test is missing)

→ Reply
